items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: What kind of bond forms between a ligand and a central metal ion in a complex?
items.correct: A dative (coordinate) covalent bond — the ligand donates a lone pair into an empty orbital on the metal.
items.misconception: The ligand forms an ionic bond with the metal because the metal is positively charged.
items.random_wrong: The ligand shares electrons equally with the metal as a normal covalent bond.
items.why: Students see the +ve metal and assume electrostatic ionic bonding, missing that ligands donate lone pairs into vacant d-orbitals.
The answer examiners want is: Bloch and Purcell 1946. “Watson and Crick 1953” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Typical O-H stretch in IR appears at:
items.why: Correct answer: 3200-3600 cm-1. “1700 cm-1” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Bloch and Purcell first demonstrated NMR?
items.why: Exact answer: 1946 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
